content: If a calibration fit makes an existing test fail, do NOT widen the test — the gate is probably right and the fit wrong. Run `git log -L <start>,<end>:<file>` on the assertion line first: if the gate's threshold was loosened by the same commit that introduced the constant you're now doubting, you've found your bug. Equally, when a fitted constant only ever "fixes" one data point, verify that data point is not itself corrupt before trusting it.
context: ISS-008 (2026-07-16). `kDryGain` was an unphysical per-path scalar that multiplied the dry/wet ratio by up to +20.5 dB. Two things let it survive: (1) the commit that added it (cef46ff) ALSO widened the very gate that contradicted it — V2IntegrationTest's dry-path check went from a correct ±12 dB "near-unity" band to a 35 dB-wide +5..+40 dB window, because the fudge forced the dry path to +24.66 dB when the circuit says +4.18 dB; (2) it was fit to zero the level error of a single capture that turned out to be CORRUPT (ISS-011 — it carried LESS HF energy than its own full-wet siblings, impossible with 50% of a bare-wire dry tap in the mix). `git log -L 188,200:tests/V2IntegrationTest.cpp` exposed the widened gate in one command, after which the whole story fell out. Removing kDryGain improved every partial-blend capture (V2 BL0.90 FR rms 10.15 -> 3.51 dB) and was neutral on every full-wet one. CLAUDE.md already carried the sibling rule "a capture-fit must never silently erase a schematic-verification gate" — the same failure mode recurring, so it is worth a standing check.
title: When a fit fails a gate, suspect the fit — `git log -L` the gate line
